text: The surgeon uses two atraumatic graspers to gently reduce the contents of a large indirect inguinal scrotal hernia. The omentum is carefully pulled down into the abdomen, assisted by external pressure on the scrotum to facilitate the reduction without causing bleeding.

The surgeon begins dissection at the medial umbilical ligament using an ipsilateral port approach. The harmonic scalpel is used to create a preperitoneal pocket, extending six centimeters from the outer margin of the deep ring at the ten o'clock position. The Teflon jaw of the harmonic scalpel is positioned towards the abdominal wall to avoid damaging the inferior epigastric vessel, while separating the peritoneum from the transversalis fascia.

The surgeon makes a preperitoneal incision starting from the medial umbilical ligament, extending six centimeters lateral to the outer margin of the defect at the ten o'clock position. Care is taken to avoid injuring the inferior epigastric vessel by positioning the Teflon jaw of the instrument towards the abdominal wall.

The surgeon begins separating the peritoneum from the transversal fascia lateral to the inferior epigastric vessel, focusing on the area known as the triangle of pain. Blunt dissection is used to carefully separate the areolar tissue, aided by insufflated gas. The lateral dissection is nearly complete.

The surgeon performs blunt dissection medial to the hernia sac, pushing the bladder inferiorly and medially to open the space of Retzius and visualize the Cooper's ligament. The sac is separated from the vas deferens and spermatic vessels in the context of a complete inguinal scrotal hernia.

The surgeon uses two atraumatic graspers to perform blunt dissection, applying inferomedial traction to visualize the Cooper ligament. The bladder is pushed inferiorly and medially to enter the space of radius. The sac is carefully separated from the pseudo sac by pushing the transversal fascia away.

In the case of a large inguinal scrotal hernia, the surgeon cuts the sac due to dense adhesions, leaving part of it in the scrotum. The transverse fascia is visible above, while the sac is below. The sac is pulled inferomedially, and the transverse fascia is pushed upward. An assistant holds the testes to prevent them from being pulled up to the deep ring. The annular space is visible as the transverse fascia is pushed up and the sac descends.

The surgeon separates the vas deferens and spermatic vessel from the hernia sac, with the vas positioned medially and the spermatic vessel laterally. A window is created between the sac and cord structures, and the harmonic scalpel is used to transect the sac when it is inseparable. The complete inguinal scrotal hernia is addressed by cutting the sac.

The surgeon enlarges the preperitoneal pocket by separating the tissue further, revealing the spermatic vessel laterally and the vas deferens medially. The dissection occurs over the triangle of doom, with the spermatic vessel and vas deferens clearly visible. The surgeon performs lateral dissection to expand the pocket, and occasionally introduces the telescope into the hernia sac to visualize the open transversalis fascia.

The entire hernia sac is separated, with no visible defects or bleeding on the peritoneal side. The sigmoid colon is seen emerging with the peritoneum. The spermatic vessel is positioned laterally, slightly separated from the peritoneum. Inside the hernia defect, the cut part of the sac is visible, indicating an indirect inguinal scrotal hernia. The triangle of doom is identified, showing the spermatic vessel and vas deferens. The Cooper ligament is visible, along with the triangle of pain. Above, the transversus fascia and arc of the transversus abdominal muscle are seen, along with the inferior epigastric vessel.

The surgeon completes the dissection of the hernia sac, clearly visualizing the spermatic vessel and inferior epigastric vessel. A mesh is spread over the triangle of dome, triangle of pain, and trapezoid of disaster, ensuring it extends six centimeters beyond the defect margins. The mesh is centered over the defect, and tackers are used to fix it medially to the Cooper ligament and laterally to the transversus abdominis muscle and rectus abdominis, avoiding the inferior epigastric vessel. The peritoneum is then used to cover the mesh, and gas pressure is reduced to facilitate this process.
